vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2013, 2017 and 2018 widely circulating. Producer publishes per-vintage technical data: e.g. 2013 declared at 11% abv, 155.7 g/L residual sugar, 7.9 g/L total acidity; 2017 cited as a textbook botrytis year with three weeks of morning mists in October; 2018 record-breaking warm dry vintage with sporadic showers driving noble rot.
sub_region: Mád / Mezőzombor (estate vineyards on volcanic and clay-loess soils)
classification: Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos (legal minimum 120 g/L residual sugar under post-2013 PDO rules; on the label this is the second-highest Puttonyos classification below 6 Puttonyos and Aszú Eszencia)
rs_level: ~150-160 g/L typical (155.7 g/L declared on 2013 vintage; minimum 120 g/L for 5 Puttonyos)
selection_notes: Definitive Tokaji Aszú 5 Puttonyos anchor for any P3 sweet flight. Royal Tokaji is the founding modern post-1990 producer (Hugh Johnson + investors). Use to differentiate Tokaji from Sauternes — note the Furmint + Hárslevelű blend, far higher acidity (7-9 g/L vs Sauternes ~4-5 g/L), botrytis berries macerated INTO fermenting base wine rather than vinified together, ageing in 300/500 L Hungarian oak fudders not Bordeaux 225 L barriques. Perfect foil to Coutet Barsac in a botrytis cross-country flight or alongside an Egon Müller BA in a sweetness-vs-mechanism trio.

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; recent classic vintages include 2015, 2016, 2017 and 2019. Owned and managed by the Baly family; Baron Philippe de Rothschild S.A. holds exclusive worldwide distribution.
sub_region: Sauternes-Barsac (commune of Barsac, between the Garonne and Ciron rivers)
classification: Premier Cru Classé (1855 Bordeaux Classification, Sauternes-Barsac section — alongside Climens, Suduiraut, Rieussec, Coutet etc.; below the lone Premier Cru Supérieur d'Yquem)
rs_level: sweet (Barsac Premier Cru style typically 110-150 g/L residual sugar)
selection_notes: Mid-tier 1er Cru Classé Sauternes anchor — deliberately NOT d'Yquem or Guiraud (the two most-cited names) to add diversity. Coutet sits in Barsac sub-appellation (clay-limestone, knife-edge acidity hinted in the name 'couteau'), 75% Sémillon/23% Sauv Blanc/2% Muscadelle, 38-44 ha, 18 months in 70-100% new French oak, average 9 hL/ha yields, manual harvest in multiple tries. Use as: (i) the Bordeaux/Sauternes reference against a Tokaji Aszú or Mosel BA in a botrytis trio; (ii) part of a Sémillon-led spread (vs Hunter Valley Tyrrell's Vat 1); (iii) Barsac-specific question (linking with 1855 classification).

vintage_guidance: Produced only in exceptional botrytis vintages and in tiny quantities. The 2024 release is the first Scharzhofberger Beerenauslese since 2015 — these wines are extreme rarities. Recent BA-producing years include 2003, 2005, 2007, 2010, 2015, 2024. Distributed exclusively through select merchants and the VDP Grosser Ring auction in Trier; estate does not sell direct.
sub_region: Wiltingen / Saar — Scharzhofberg vineyard (devonian blue slate, south-facing, considered Grand Cru equivalent of the Saar)
classification: VDP.Grosse Lage — Prädikat: Beerenauslese (legal Oechsle minimum ~110-128° depending on region; botrytis berry-by-berry selection)
rs_level: sweet (BA band typically 150-220 g/L for Mosel BA; Egon Müller 2024 noted as lower-band BA-style but still classified as BA after acid-stopped fermentation)
selection_notes: The apex of the Mosel Prädikat ladder — a true MW-exam curveball when paired with Tokaji and Sauternes in a botrytis trio. Note the contrast: Mosel BA is 100% Riesling, fermentation arrests naturally at 7-8% abv (vs ~13.5% Sauternes / ~11% Tokaji), residual sugar similar or higher, but acidity is the highest of the three (~9-11 g/L) — this acid-sugar tension is the Mosel signature. Deliberately distinct from the Dr Loosen and JJ Prüm entries already in the bank (Kabinett/Spätlese tier). Egon Müller produces these only in exceptional years; the 2024 is the first BA since 2015. Source confidence is medium because the estate does not publish a public BA tech sheet — sourcing relies on detailed Lobenberg retailer notes plus producer biography pages and JancisRobinson coverage.

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2024 and 2025 currently in market. 2024 noted as exceptionally dry — the driest Marlborough season in 30+ years — slowing ripening and helping retain balance. Founded 1985 by David Hohnen of Cape Mentelle, now owned by LVMH.
sub_region: Wairau Valley (Rapaura, Renwick, Brancott Valley sub-regions) — key blocks Estate, Motukawa, Widow's Block, Red Shed, Mustang
selection_notes: The single most globally recognized Marlborough Sauvignon Blanc — founded 1985 by David Hohnen, now LVMH-owned, set the world standard for the Marlborough thiolic style. Mandatory anchor for any cross-country Sauv Blanc spread (vs Sancerre, vs Chilean Errazuriz already in bank, vs Pessac-Léognan, vs Marlborough Bordeaux blend). Wairau Valley gravelly free-draining soils, exceptional sunshine hours, marked diurnals → passionfruit/gooseberry/lime/white nectarine signature with sea-salt edge. Use to contrast with a flinty/reductive Sancerre or a textural Greywacke alternative; or as a commercial benchmark foil at the value end of an NZ flight.

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2023 and 2024 widely available. Producer publishes per-vintage tech sheets with full analytical detail. 2024 reviewed by JancisRobinson team as 'slightly slimmer and more lithe' vs 2023. First vintage of this wine: 1994.
sub_region: Blend across Lunlunta (Maipú, ~920 m), Gualtallary (Tupungato, ~1,450 m), and El Cepillo/La Consulta/Eugenio Bustos (Uco Valley, ~1,090 m)
classification: Estate-tier cuvée (Catena's mainstream multi-vineyard blend; one tier above the regional Alamos line and below Catena Alta, Catena Zapata Malbec Argentino, and Adrianna Vineyard parcel wines)
selection_notes: The mainstream/value Malbec anchor — deliberately the entry-level Catena Estate cuvée (~$15-25 retail) to create a price-and-quality ladder against the Catena Adrianna Vineyard River Malbec already in the bank (~$190-250). Same producer, same variety, same broad region, but the Estate blend smooths multiple altitudes (Lunlunta lowland + Gualtallary high + La Consulta mid) into a softer commercial expression vs Adrianna's single high-altitude parcel intensity. Excellent for: (i) Argentina price ladder questions; (ii) cross-country Malbec spreads (Catena vs Cahors Cosse-Maisonneuve / Lagrézette); (iii) Mendoza commercial anchor in any P2 reds flight. ID deliberately distinct from the existing Adrianna entry.

vintage_guidance: Non-vintage / current-release blended NV style. World's #1 Vinho Verde by volume per producer; distributed in 70+ countries. Tech sheet publishes 80 hL/ha average yield, 6.5 g/L tartaric total acidity, pH 2.8-3.4, 11 g/L residual sugar.
sub_region: Vinho Verde DOC (sourced across the region via grower partnerships into the Aveleda vinification centre)
rs_level: 11 g/L (just off-dry in profile, technically dry by EU labelling)
selection_notes: The default value white anchor — distinct from the Soalheiro Alvarinho premium entry already in the bank (which is single-variety, premium Monção). Casal Garcia is the world's #1-selling Vinho Verde, a multi-grape blend (Trajadura, Loureiro, Pedernã, Azal) at 9.5% abv, 11 g/L RS, slight petillance, ~$8 retail. Use as: (i) the commercial value-price floor in any P1 white flight; (ii) the cheap NV-style anchor in a Portugal flight vs Soalheiro / Niepoort; (iii) a low-alcohol curveball where the slight spritz and 9.5% abv directs candidates away from neutral varieties like Pinot Grigio; (iv) the unmistakable signal of granite-soils, multi-grape Vinho Verde — a classic IMW broad-commercial-anchor question.

vintage_guidance: Use current or recent vintage; 2020, 2022, 2023 widely circulating. Producer publishes a tech sheet; 2020 importer (Vintus) PDF cites traditional long maceration in stainless steel with twice-daily pump-overs, and 24-30 months in neutral oak (this differs slightly from producer page which states 18 months in foudres — both descriptions are sourced; difference is between 'collection' bottlings vs vintage variation). ~4.5 million bottles produced annually.
sub_region: Côtes du Rhône (broad regional sourcing across the southern Rhône; Guigal house in Ampuis, northern Rhône)
selection_notes: The reference value red anchor — Guigal Côtes du Rhône Rouge is the most-shipped Rhône red in the world (~4.5 million bottles/year, retails ~$13-18). Deliberately Syrah-led (50% Syrah / 40% Grenache / 10% Mourvèdre) — atypical for a Southern Rhône blend which is more commonly Grenache-dominant — and aged 18-30 months in neutral oak / foudres, far longer than competitors at the price. This is a classic MW exam curveball: the structure says serious producer, the price band says value, the blend ratio whispers 'northern Rhône influence on a southern Rhône AOC.' Use as: (i) the value-end anchor in a P2 reds flight; (ii) Rhône AOC ladder question (CdR → CdR Villages → Gigondas → Châteauneuf); (iii) cross-country GSM/Syrah-blend comparison (vs South African Stellenbosch, vs Aussie Barossa GSM, vs Spanish Garnacha-led).
